phase1.output:
int to double: 100.0
double to int: 3
Hex: 255
Binary: 10
phase1.explanation:
Type casting is converting one type to another:

Widening (implicit): int → double, char → int
• Happens automatically
• Safe — no data loss

Narrowing (explicit): double → int
• Must use (targetType) cast
• Can lose precision (3.14159 becomes 3)

Literals are fixed values written directly in code:
• 0xFF = 255 in hexadecimal (starts with 0x)
• 0b1010 = 10 in binary (starts with 0b)
• 'A' = char literal
• "Hello" = String literal

Remember: String is NOT a primitive — it's a class, but Java treats it specially with literals!

phase2.errorMessage:
CastingExample.java:8: error: incompatible types: possible lossy conversion from double to int
        int roughPi = pi;
                      ^
1 error
phase2.explanation:
Java won't let you assign a double to an int without explicit casting because you might lose the decimal part!

To fix it, you must use explicit casting: (int) pi

This is Java protecting you from accidentally losing data. Always use (type) when narrowing!

phase3.prompt:
Write code that:
1. Declares a double variable with value 9.87
2. Casts it to an int (prints the result)
3. Declares an int variable with value 42
4. Widens it to a double (prints the result)
dialogueHints: For the double to int conversion, use (int) in front of the variable.
Widening is automatic — just assign the int to a double variable.
Make sure to print both converted values!
phase3.openingDialogue: Let's practice playing with types — don't drop any data! 🎪
